name: The Productivity Institute — PIP062 (Sept 2025)
description: Social care expenditure rose 45% while output only increased 5%
statistic: According to ONS data analysed in PIP062, indexed measures of nominal government expenditure on adult social care rose by 45% from financial year 2016/17 to 2023/24, while the output index (measuring the total number of people served) remained broadly stable between 2016/17 and 2022/23 and only increased in the final years, implying an overall growth of just 5% over the same period. This means spending nearly doubled in real terms while the number of people actually receiving care barely grew — a stark illustration of the productivity crisis in adult social care. The calendar year (CY) estimates show a more pronounced decline in adult social care productivity across the UK of nearly 9% between 2010 and 2022, compared to 2.5% in the financial year (FY) estimates for England. The input rise was 17% in CY data versus 8% in FY data. The paper — 'Productivity and Responsible AI in Adult Social Care' — explores how AI and responsible technology deployment could address this productivity gap, noting that the conventional ONS methodology assumes zero productivity growth in public services by equating input costs with output value. Authors: Joel Hoskins and Bart van Ark.
credibility: Academic/research institute. The Productivity Institute is an ESRC-funded research centre based at the University of Manchester, working across academia, business, and policy. Bart van Ark is the Managing Director and a Professor of Productivity Studies. The underlying expenditure and output data are from the Office for National Statistics (ONS), which is the UK's national statistical authority. The analysis is peer-reviewed through the Institute's working paper series.
applicability: Directly care sector — specifically adult social care productivity in England and the UK. The analysis uses ONS public service productivity data for adult social care, examining the relationship between government spending and care output.
citable: Yes — publicly available working paper with clear attribution. Standard citation: 'Hoskins, J. and van Ark, B. (2025), Productivity and Responsible AI in Adult Social Care, Productivity Insights Paper No. 062, The Productivity Institute.' The ONS underlying data is also separately citable.
website_placement: Pain point section — this is a powerful macro-level stat that frames the entire sector problem. 'The UK spends 45% more on social care than it did in 2016/17, but only 5% more people are receiving care. The system isn't broken — it's just massively inefficient.' Works well as a data visualisation: a line chart showing the diverging expenditure and output curves. Also effective in thought leadership content, blog posts, and the gated state-of-sector report. Can be used in the hero section as a provocative opener: 'Social care spending up 45%. Output up 5%. Something has to change.'
emotional_vs_rational: Primarily rational — this is a macro-economic productivity argument that appeals to analytically minded buyers, policymakers, investors, and operations directors. However, the emotional undercurrent is powerful: the implication that billions of pounds are being absorbed by an inefficient system while care workers are underpaid and residents are underserved. The gap between 45% and 5% is so dramatic it creates an emotional reaction even among data-driven audiences. For care home owners, it validates their lived experience: 'We're spending more every year but not serving more people.'
clarica_differentiator: Very strong differentiator. This is perhaps the most strategically important stat for Clarica's positioning. It provides an evidence-based, academically credible argument that the social care sector has a structural productivity problem — and that AI is the most promising solution. The paper itself (PIP062) examines how responsible AI can address this productivity gap, directly supporting Clarica's mission. Clarica can frame its services as part of the solution to a nationally recognised problem: 'The Productivity Institute found that care spending rose 45% while output grew just 5%. We're here to close that gap with AI.' This elevates Clarica from a service provider to a mission-driven agency tackling a systemic challenge.
refresh_frequency: The underlying ONS productivity data is updated annually (next release expected mid-2026). The Productivity Institute working paper series publishes new papers continuously. A follow-up or update to PIP062 may be published if the authors extend their analysis, but no confirmed schedule. The 45% vs 5% figures cover a specific period (2016/17 to 2023/24) and will evolve as new annual data is added.
comparison_pair: The core comparison: expenditure up 45% vs. output up 5% (2016/17 to 2023/24). This 40-percentage-point gap is the central narrative. Additional comparison: adult social care productivity declined nearly 9% between 2010 and 2022 using calendar year estimates — compared to broader public sector productivity which also stagnated but less dramatically. For Clarica: 'If spending 45% more only delivers 5% more care, the answer isn't more money — it's smarter processes. AI is the productivity lever that social care has been missing.' Can be paired with the GOV.UK stat about 30 million admin hours saved through digital records to show the trajectory: digitisation was the first step, AI is the next.
